title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Universe Beneath Our Feet
content.text: When we think of biodiversity, we often picture rainforests or coral reefs. Rarely do we look down at the dirt beneath our boots. Yet, a single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more living organisms than there are people on Earth. This complex community of fungi, bacteria, and insects is the engine of life on our planet. || (41) || [41] Despite its importance, soil is one of the most misunderstood and neglected resources in the world [/41]. For decades, we have treated it as an inert medium—something that just holds plants up—rather than a living, breathing ecosystem. This industrial approach to agriculture has led to a global crisis of soil degradation. || (42) || [42] The scale of the loss is staggering [/42]. According to the UN, we are losing fertile soil at a rate of 24 billion tonnes a year. If current trends continue, some scientists warn that we may only have 60 harvests left. || (43) || [43] At the heart of this subterranean world is the 'Mycorrhizal network' [/43]. These are vast webs of fungal threads that connect the roots of different plants and trees. Through this 'Wood Wide Web,' plants can share nutrients, warn each other of pests, and even support younger saplings in the shade. || (44) || [44] This symbiotic relationship is the secret to a forest’s resilience [/44]. The fungi provide the plants with minerals like phosphorus and nitrogen, which they extract from the rocks, while the plants provide the fungi with sugar produced through photosynthesis. || (45) || [45] However, our use of chemical fertilizers is breaking these ancient bonds [/45]. When we pump the soil with artificial nutrients, plants stop 'trading' with the fungi. Over time, the fungal networks wither away, leaving the plants dependent on chemicals and far more vulnerable to disease and drought. || (46) || [46] The good news is that soil can be restored [/46]. Through 'regenerative agriculture'—practices like no-till farming, cover cropping, and composting—we can bring the biology of the soil back to life. By healing the ground, we can sequester more carbon, grow more nutritious food, and ensure the survival of the 'universe' beneath our feet.

paragraphs.A: [42] This is largely due to the use of heavy machinery and intensive plowing [/42]. These practices destroy the delicate structure of the soil, making it easily swept away by wind and rain, eventually turning productive land into dust.
paragraphs.B: [44] It is an exchange that has evolved over 400 million years [/44]. Without it, the colonization of land by plants might never have happened, and our world would look very different today.
paragraphs.C: [41] It performs essential 'ecosystem services' that we take for granted [/41]. Soil filters our water, regulates our climate by storing carbon, and provides the foundation for almost everything we eat.
paragraphs.D: [43] This hidden infrastructure is only now being mapped by scientists [/43]. It turns out that a forest is not just a collection of individual trees, but a cooperative society where information is constantly being exchanged.
paragraphs.E: [46] These methods focus on nurturing the microbes rather than just feeding the plants [/46]. When the biological community is healthy, the soil becomes a sponge that can hold massive amounts of water and carbon dioxide.
paragraphs.F: In urban environments, soil is often buried under concrete and asphalt. This 'sealing' of the earth prevents water from soaking in and can lead to increased urban flooding and the death of city trees.
paragraphs.G: [45] This leads to a vicious cycle of dependency [/45]. As the soil becomes more sterile, farmers are forced to use even more chemicals to get the same yield, further damaging the very ecosystem they rely on.

questions.explanation: C detalla los 'ecosystem services' del suelo, expandiendo la idea de por qué es una comunidad compleja antes de mencionar su descuido.
A explica las causas físicas de la pérdida de suelo (maquinaria y arado) que se menciona en el texto principal.
D se refiere a la 'hidden infrastructure', que encaja con la descripción de la red micorrízica (fungal threads).
B habla del intercambio evolutivo de 400 millones de años, lo cual refuerza la descripción de la relación simbiótica.
G describe el 'vicious cycle' de dependencia química, que es la consecuencia directa del uso de fertilizantes mencionado.
E describe los métodos de la 'regenerative agriculture' (microbes over plants) mencionados al final del texto.
